frameworks.name: latent group structure inference
frameworks.one_line: learners infer unobservable social groups from choice patterns to guide imitation

frameworks.name: dyadic similarity heuristic
frameworks.one_line: influence driven only by pairwise overlap in past choices

frameworks.name: computational model of social learning
frameworks.one_line: formal model grouping individuals by choices to weight social influence

frameworks.name: infant preference understanding
frameworks.one_line: developmental social cognition reinterpreted via latent group inference

core_question: How do people decide whose choices to follow when dyadic similarity alone cannot distinguish informants?
key_assumptions: learners group individuals (including self) by choice patterns
latent social structure is inferable from observable choices
group membership modulates the weight of social influence
manipulations: degree of choice overlap between self and others
configurations equating dyadic overlap while differing in latent group structure
key_findings: choices are more influenced by others whose past choices overlap more with one's own
influence diverges from dyadic overlap when latent group structure differs
model predicts behavior in cases where pairwise similarity is equated
interpretations: social influence reflects inferred group structure, not just dyadic similarity
infant developmental changes can be reframed as latent group inference
